headline: Geopolitical Fog Hangs Over Tuesday's Open as Ceasefire Hopes Temper Oil Risk
body_html:
Futures point to a cautious open on Tuesday after Wall Street closed Monday's session in broad retreat — only 146 of 501 names in our universe finished higher, with seven of eight tracked sectors in the red. Homebuilders led losses, shedding 1.82%, while industrials, energy, and healthcare each fell more than 1%. Semiconductors were the lone bright spot, edging up 0.46%, with connectivity and test-equipment names among the session's biggest individual winners.

Overnight, the geopolitical backdrop remains the dominant market driver. Mediators have floated a US-Iran ceasefire proposal, pulling crude off its highs and helping Asian equities stabilize after Monday's pressure. Gold rose roughly 1% as investors balanced diplomatic optimism against persistent risk, and European bank shares are being watched closely following reports of a potential lending and trading windfall. A tanker crew reportedly abandoned ship after a projectile strike in the Strait of Hormuz, keeping shipping-lane anxiety alive even as oil retreated.

At the open, traders will weigh whether the ceasefire signal has enough substance to sustain Monday's late oil pullback or whether fresh Houthi escalation — including a declared naval blockade against Saudi Arabia — reasserts the geopolitical premium. Energy and industrials bear watching given Monday's weakness; any further softening in crude could provide selective relief there.

briefs.headline: Ceasefire Feelers Cool Oil's Climb
briefs.body: Diplomatic intermediaries have put a US-Iran truce framework on the table, pulling crude prices lower overnight and helping Asian equities recover some ground lost during Monday's session. The proposal remains unconfirmed by either side, leaving energy markets in a holding pattern ahead of any official response.

briefs.headline: Tenth Consecutive Night of US Strikes on Iran
briefs.body: American forces carried out another round of military strikes against Iran overnight, the tenth successive night of such operations. The continued tempo underscores why energy and shipping markets remain on edge even as ceasefire talks circulate, and it keeps a geopolitical risk premium embedded in oil pricing.

briefs.headline: Hormuz Tanker Incident Rattles Shipping Lanes
briefs.body: A tanker crew evacuated their vessel after a reported projectile strike in the Strait of Hormuz, according to maritime safety authority UKMTO. Traffic through the critical chokepoint has been declining on renewed US-Iran hostilities, raising freight-risk concerns for carriers and oil importers alike.

briefs.headline: Houthis Declare Naval Blockade Against Saudi Arabia
briefs.body: Yemen's Houthi movement announced a naval blockade targeting Saudi Arabia, adding another layer of complexity to Red Sea and Gulf shipping routes already strained by months of regional conflict. Analysts note that practical workarounds exist but could meaningfully raise transport costs if the blockade is enforced.

briefs.headline: European Banks Poised for Earnings Tailwind
briefs.body: European financial institutions are set to report a significant boost to profits driven by stronger lending margins and elevated trading revenues, according to reports ahead of the region's earnings disclosures. The prospect lifted sentiment in overnight European trading and could spill into financials-sector positioning stateside at the open.

briefs.headline: Monday's Standouts: e.l.f. Beauty Surges, Solar Names Slide
briefs.body: e.l.f. Beauty topped our universe's leaderboard Monday with an 8.09% advance, while Inspire Medical Systems and Credo Technology each gained more than 4%. On the downside, STAAR Surgical dropped 9.34% and SolarEdge fell 9.09%, with Bloom Energy close behind at -8.33%, continuing pressure on clean-energy names.
